title: Noise cancellation by superposition of channels and superactivation of quantum capacity: Experimental realization by NMR
authors: Deepika Bhargava, Arijit Chatterjee, Vishal Varma, T. S. Mahesh
abstract: Noisy quantum channels degrade quantum resources such as coherence and entanglement and hence pose challenges for realizing quantum technologies. Coherent control of noisy channels allows us to minimize their effects on the quantum system. Here we achieve the cancellation of two noisy quantum channels by superposing their corresponding Stinespring dilation unitaries. We first arrive at conditions under which superposition of channels results in a valid quantum channel. We then consider superposing two dephasing channels and observe their destructive interference, thereby effectively recovering the quantum coherence. On superposing two zero-capacity depolarizing channels, we show superactivation of quantum capacity. We experimentally realize the cancellation of two dephasing channels using a three-qubit NMR register. Furthermore, using a five-qubit NMR register, we realize the cancellation of two depolarization channels and demonstrate superactivation of quantum capacity.
summary: This paper demonstrates a novel technique to combat quantum noise by superposing multiple noisy quantum channels. Using NMR spectroscopy on multi-qubit registers, the authors experimentally achieved the recovery of quantum coherence and demonstrated 'superactivation' of quantum capacity when combining zero-capacity noise sources. This work validates coherent control methods for enhancing quantum information processing capabilities.
main_problem: The research addresses how noise degrades quantum resources by proposing a method to cancel these detrimental effects through the superposition of noisy quantum channels.
main_result: The authors successfully demonstrated the cancellation of two dephasing channels using NMR and achieved 'superactivation' of quantum capacity by superposing two zero-capacity depolarizing channels in a five-qubit NMR setup.
method: They theoretically derived conditions for valid channel superposition using Stinespring dilations, which was then experimentally realized using controlled unitary operations on multi-qubit Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) registers.
model_or_system: The system involves quantum channels modeled via Pauli maps and implemented experimentally on NMR registers of varying sizes (3-qubit for dephasing, 5-qubit for depolarization). The control mechanism relies on superposing Stinespring dilation unitaries acting on an ancillary qubit.
key_observables: Quantum coherence recovery ($\langle\sigma_iangle$ expectation values), normalization factor ($\gamma$), effective error probabilities ($p'_i$), and the quantum capacity of the resulting channel.
important_parameters: The superposition parameter $\alpha$, which controls the mixture between the two channels, is crucial for observing interference effects.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes ancillary qubits are initialized in pure states ($ho_A = |0_nangle\langle 0_n|_A$) for simplicity, although extensions to mixed states are possible.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the quantum circuits used for superposition (using control qubits and postselection) and plot key observables like $\langle\sigma_iangle$, normalization factor $\gamma$, and effective channel strength $p$ as a function of the superposition parameter $\alpha$.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from theoretical derivation of valid superposed channels to analyzing specific cases (dephasing, depolarizing). It then details the experimental realization using NMR for both coherence recovery and capacity enhancement.
why_interesting: This work provides a concrete, experimentally validated platform (NMR) for implementing advanced quantum information protocols—specifically noise mitigation via channel superposition—which is highly relevant to open quantum systems research.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Illustrating the cancellation of two channels when super- posed.

Figure 2. (a) Realizing superposition of channels on system S via a superposed unitary acting on a larger space, followed by partial trace of ancillary register A. (b) Full quantum circuit for realizing superposition of channels via two Steinspring dilation unitaries U and eU controlled by qubit C, followed by postselection and partial trace. (c) Quantum circuit for finding the normalization factor γ. It involves a conditional measurement of diagonal elements as shown.

Figure 3. NMR realization of superposition of two dephasing channels. (a) Pulse sequence implementing control unitaries U(p) and U(ep) of the quantum circuit in Fig. 2 (b). Here, 1H, 19F, and 13C are the system, ancilla, and control qubits respectively. White and black rectangles represent x- and y-pulses with pulse angles mentioned on top of them. Vertical lines joining qubits a and b represent angles Θ ∈{π, π/2, ±β, 2θ} realized by coupling evolution delays Θ/(4πJab). For negative Θ, a delay of 3|Θ|/(4πJab) was used. For the dephasing channel with strength p, β = tan−1 p

Figure 4. Experimental realization of superposition of two depolar- izing channels of strengths p = 0.55 and ep = 0.7. (a) Molecular structure of BTFBz and table of resonance offsets νi (diagonal ele- ments in Hz), effective coupling constants Jij + 2Dij (off-diagonal elements in Hz), and relaxation time constants. (b-d) The effective strength p of superposed channel, the normalization factor γ, and expectation value ⟨σ3⟩, plotted versus the superposition parameter α. The lines and markers represent theory and experimental data. In all the cases, P1-error bars [36] of length one standard deviation were calculated considering RF inhomogeneity and thermal noise.
